title: 2025/07/17 - TI
displayTitle: 2025-07-16 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee - Report of the Controller and Auditor-General: Regulating vehicle safety inspections
topic: Report of the Controller and Auditor-General: Regulating vehicle safety inspections
description: Thursday 17 July 2025 Transport and Infrastructure Committee Report of the Controller and Auditor-General: Regulating vehicle safety inspections Hearing of evidence Office of the Auditor-General Kate Williams, Senior Performance Auditor Jason Hewett, Manager, Performance Audit Clint Ramoo, Audit director, Audit New Zealand
descriptionSummary: Report of the Controller and Auditor-General: Regulating vehicle safety inspections. Office of the Auditor-General. Kate Williams, Senior Performance Auditor

chunks.text: Then I'll pass over to Kate. So, um, Miranda Koto. My name's Jason Hewitt, manager of Performance Audits. Um, joined here today, um, by Kate Williams, the audit lead for this piece of work. Um, and, uh, clin, uh, audit director from Audit New Zealand. So I'll, that's, that's my part so far. So I'll pass you on to Kate. Thanks, Jason. Yeah, so I'm just gonna talk a bit about, um, why we did this audit, what we found, and then we can move on to any questions that you've got.

Sure. Um, we did this audit because vehicle safety is important. Um, if you've got a car, you'll know about getting a warrant of fitness, but even if you don't have a car, you know, you use our roads, you might go on the bus, you might bike. So you wanna know that the vehicles on our roads are safe. We looked at this audit, or for this audit, we looked at how well, um, NZTA regulates the vehicle inspectors and inspecting organizations that carry out warrants and certificates of fitness.

We looked at how they, how they appoint inspectors. We looked at how they detect and respond to non-compliance. So when they're not doing the job properly, and we looked at how they, um, monitor and report on the work that they do. Back in 2018, a car passenger died when their seatbelt failed in an accident.

The, um, the car had been recently checked and given a warrant of fitness, but it turned out that, um, the car had not been inspected properly. As a result of this. NZTA carried out some, um, investigations and found that they had what they called a regulatory failure in terms of the warrant and certificates of fitness. They found there were, I think about 850 cases of non-compliance that they hadn't followed up, and about 150 of those, they deemed to be, um, high priority.

So after that, there were some reviews, a lot of changes were made, and, and we came in, um, some years after that to do the audit. What we found was that NCTA now has much better processes in place to detect non-compliance and to respond quickly and consistently when they find non-compliance. And that was a, you know, a very good finding.

But we did find some areas that they also need to improve. So one of those is around it. Um, the regulation and warrants and certificates of fitness still uses a lot of quite, um, outdated IT systems. So the main database that they use, um, for the, for the inspections, there's an old access database. A lot of their work is based on the spreadsheets for tracking their work and things like that.

We also found that the vehicle standards themselves could be easier to find and understand, and we thought that there could be clearer links between the work or, or the impact of warrant certificates of fitness and the impact that has on overall road safety, because without those links, it makes it hard to see what the impact of that work is. We also talked a bit about the future.

So with a warrant or certificate of fitness at the moment, they take a vehicle and they do pretty much a physical check of it. You know, they sound the horn, they test the brakes, they have a look to see if there's any rust on the undercarriage. But more and more cars now have got very modern, um, safety features, which are based on advanced technology. And those things aren't looked at in a warrant or certificate of fitness.

And at the moment, you know, nobody's really looking at that. So that's something that needs to be dealt with in the future. NZTA and the Ministry of Transport are aware of that, and there is work starting, they're starting to think about, um, what that might look like and how we might have assurance about vehicle safety in the future. Just for the committee, um, there's a few things the committee might think about.

As a result of this audit, we made five recommendations and our report, the committee might like to ask NZTA, um, to come in and talk about their progress, um, against those recommendations for the work being done about the future of vehicle safety testing. The Ministry of Transport might be better placed to talk about that work. Um, also some of our findings might apply to other regulators like maritime, um, maritime New Zealand.

Um, there might be questions for those regulators such as how are they using data and analytics to support their work, um, whether they have got clear outcomes and can see what the outcomes are from their regulatory work and you know, what they're doing about looking to the future to see how things might need to change as technology changes. So thank you. We're happy to answer any questions that you've got. Thanks, Kate.

Um, Dan, you had fir you were first up. Oh yeah. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, really good. Interesting report. Uh, thank you. Um, was this, I'm just quite kinda understand in terms of why you chose this area. Was it, um, did you have inquiries in your office to, to look at this or, Um, there wasn't any particular trigger?

Um, yeah, it was just an area that we thought was, was important, something that we hadn't looked at before. Yeah. Fantastic. The other thing is, um, I heard you talk about technology and definitely like modern cars these days. Mm-hmm. Just more like, um, modern lap laptops on wheels. So, um, you know, I know my two vehicles have a lot of tech that just regulates even the, the handbrake now is electronic rather than physical.

Mm-hmm. So how do, how do inspectors build that into their regime? Um, you know that we are moving to basically electronics controlling most of Yeah. The car system. So there's, there's a couple of things there. So if it's a, um, an electronic thing that's replacing an existing feature, then I think they would test it to some extent if it functions in the same way.

So one of the examples that they talked to us about was how some cars, um, now rather than having mirrors, they have cameras. And this was a bit of a problem to start with because the regulations were very rigid and said they needed to have a mirror. And they've now had to change that to say they need to have a mirror or something that does the same job as a mirror that can see behind the car.

So they can change the regulations to some extent, but they don't go as far as testing the actual hardware or software of it. They just would check that you can see behind the vehicle. But where there are, are new features like, you know, lane keep assist or something like that, that is not checked as part of a, a warrant of fitness. Mm. Which would be concerned given sooner or later those might start having faults and Yeah. And there's more and more vehicles have them, it's gonna be more and more of a, of an issue.

Yes. And so that's why, you know, the Ministry of Transport is already looking into that. And in terms of legislative change, I mean, I guess we've got clunky old, older legislation of, did your organization come up with any recommendations on the legislative front or, I think the main recommendation was around just really making, um, it easier to change the leg legislation.

Mm-hmm. So, you know, um, even a quite a minor change, because the regulations are secondary legislation, there is a process to go through and so NZTA can't necessarily just make a change that makes sense easily without going through a full legislative process for that secondary legislation. Yeah, correct. Thank you. So can I just supplement you?

I'm sorry. So is that, um, so you reckon the current checklist, you know, when you do well, it's not fit for purpose anymore. Like there's certain things need to be add on to be checked as well. I don't know whether say it's fit for purpose or not, it's not really our role to say, say what our inspection should cover.

I think we are just saying that it doesn't cover those things, and that's a challenge that needs to be considered, um, by the policy makers to think about, well, what, what is the purpose of these inspections? What do they need to cover? And then how, how do you address that? Um, I think, you know, the way that, um, inspections are done at the moment and the workforce that does inspections at the moment is not necessarily the right, right.

Workforce with the right skillset to go and start testing advanced technologies, a whole different approach might be needed. So it's quite a major piece of work that's going to need to be done to look at those advanced technologies and how we get that assurance, and then also who's responsible for, for that. Sure. Thank you. Can I, Camilla, You go first, Um, just a recommendation five.

Um, and I understand about technologies changing, but you've kind of recommended there that, um, that the inspection requirements are easier to change. Do you think that that's too permissive in terms of, uh, relying on technology changes being sufficient to meet safety requirements when perhaps they might not be, uh, as, as people have said, they might have faults or errors or there might be things that that come up in the future.

Um, and do you think change is the right word, or do you think modify Is it might maybe modify? Yeah, so we are not really talking about the major changes. Um, so at the moment there are, you know, there's a rule for each part of the vehicle, um, and it just specifies it's quite functional, um, about what it needs, what it needs to do, what they need to be able to do.

Um, but sometimes maybe that doesn't work or there's a bit of a change that means it just doesn't quite make sense. And at the moment, that's quite hard to change, but I think for a more, um, for a more substantive change, then a whole different approach might be needed anyway. Yeah. Thanks. Can I have Yeah, go on. Yeah, sure. Uh, I didn't actually receive the report, so if something already written, don't just apologize for that.

Do you guys actually look into, um, people who don't actually do the inspection as well, but you know, some cars may, after they passed the wharf, like for almost one or two years, they, they still not registered or, or, or be doing the inspection. Do you actually look into that, the area as well? So for this audit, no, we didn't look at that.

So we just looked at how well the people that carry out the WFS and calfs are doing the Agile, but we didn't look at, um, how well the regulation of vehicle owners, if you like, we didn't look at that side of it. Yeah. Did did you have in the, in the process of doing this, and we just had a few questions about vehicle technology and the way that that changes, did you need to understand in any way that the way in which that vehicle technology is changing the testing processes they go through as manufacturers?

The lot, the, the longevity of the, the technology that's the, particularly the safety technology that's in the cars. Um, we didn't look at that in a lot of detail. No. I mean, it was mentioned to us, um, in passing in some of our conversations, but it wasn't really within the scope of the order to look at that. So do you think that as NZT slash MOT are looking at the way in which they might, uh, respond to this, um, they ought to be considering that what, what technology actually is capable of and what its limitations might be?

Um, I think we'd expect them to look at, you know, all aspects of the technology, um, or any, anything that's relevant. Yeah. Okay. Yeah. Cool. Um, and see the other area you, 'cause this, this really is about the inspecting, I would say inspecting the inspectors in a way, isn't it? Yeah. Yeah. So the capability of the inspectors mm-hmm.

Your view is, I mean, clearly there were some who were deficient, end of story. Um, that's, so when we, when we were talking about 850, um, cases, non-compliance, that's 850 individual cases, which might be one, one entity doing it eight wrong, 850 times could be. Yeah. Or eight 50 to what doing it mm-hmm.

One time, whatever it might be. So it's a, it's a, it's a number of, um, what, um, what, what was I gonna ask? What, what you you're basically saying is, are NZTA now as the regulator there in your view now on top of that, so that the, the industry is actually delivering more the fitnesses in a way that it should be doing.

It's doing the inspections properly, it's not letting things through the gate. Yeah. Are you reasonably comfortable with that now? Yeah, so we're reasonably reasonably comfortable that now they've got processes in place to do that. So, you know, it's a human system. People inspecting vehicles, there's always gonna be some problems you can't guarantee there'll never be any, any problems, but they've got a lot of processes in place.

Um, so one of the examples that they do is use data analysis. So they look at, um, you know, they'll look at any individuals or inspecting organizations that have got higher than average pass rates or fail rates, so they can sort of see if there's any anomalies with those and look into those in more detail. Um, from when they do find problems, they've now just got much stricter processes in place.

So all the inspectors have the same, um, all of the NZTA inspectors have the same process to follow a lot of guidance, but, you know, if you find these sorts of errors, this is how we're gonna respond, this is the sort of letter you need to send. So it's a lot more consistent. And they've got processes internally where the more serious cases of non-compliance get dealt, dealt with by a panel, The non-compliance of the Inspectors of the inspector.

So if someone's not inspecting a vehicle properly. Yeah. Yeah. And just the other thing is we, we can, we can veer from, in New Zealand or many other jurisdictions, we can veer from being a bit laissez fair to being so hard on the regulations that we fail. An enormous number of people doesn't matter whether it's, whether it's a building or whether it's a car or whether it's whatever it might be.

Um, but we gotta make sure we got the balance. In other words, we can say we're gonna make it so, so tight, the cars which are perfectly good, or vehicles which are perfectly good, actually, we, we've essentially we're failing them and we make, we're adding cost, et cetera, to the system. Are you confident that we've got that from what you've seen? That we've we are not, we're not varying from, we had a problem to going right the other way and saying, actually we're gonna create a problem in the opposite direction, be regularly safe,

but we're also economically very, very expensive. And a lot of people go, oh, you know, it's, it's very expensive. Cost of living and all that sort of thing. Very difficult for people. So in terms of the requirements for the vehicles themselves, that's not something that we looked at. Um, but it is something, you know, the sorts of things that we would expect, um, ministry of Transport to be considering when it's looking to the future, you know, how the burden, the costs, um, and, and getting that, getting the regulatory

settings on the vehicles. Correct. Now what we were looking at was the, um, inspectors, as you said, I inspecting the inspectors and the way NZTA does that now they've got a, um, sort of graded system. So depending on, you know, if they see non-compliance and it's quite minor, they might just write them a letter saying, this is what you need to fix, or that we might come back in a couple of weeks and check you fixed it right up to, um, you know, removing someone's authority to carry out inspections.

If it's something more serious Yeah. That, but that the inspect as opposed to what the inspectors are doing, then downstream to the people who are actually bringing their vehicles in. Yes. Yeah. Okay. Um, so anybody else wanna pick up a question mate before I go and do another one? Carlos? Yes. I just saw on your report, you, um, you mentioned NCTA got a safer vehicle team mm-hmm.

Managing the regulation of the vehicle inspector and I inspecting organization. Mm-hmm. Like how, how, how many people's there to, to, to do the regular check for all this, um, organizational inspector, Um, just trying to remember the number. So they've got, um, they've got I think four regional teams and there's maybe up to 10 people in each of those.

But we, so we just looked at the warrants and certificates of fitness. The Safer Vehicles team also does other types of vehicle inspections, so entry inspection of new vehicles or imported vehicles. Um, also sort of modifications. So those teams are not just doing the warrants and certificates of fitness. Um, but what they do now is for, for the vehicle inspectors and inspector organizations, they're on a three yearly cycle of, um, NZTA coming and checking them.

So it's, rather than a numbers thing, it's, it's a time thing of how frequently they'll come and look at them. And they're pretty much on top of that three yearly cycle now. Mm-hmm. Um, if there has been a case of non-compliance in the past, that might be, they might come back and look at them more quickly than that. So that, that can be one of the concern if you only got 10 people, but you got what, 3,600 organization of, of 12,000 inspector there.

So it's very hard to keep the standard consistent or, or, or keep a, a standard for everyone. Um, I mean the, the, when they go and do a site inspection, we actually went along on a couple of them to see, to see what they do. So they have got quite a fixed process that they follow. Um, everyone has, all the NZTA inspectors have to follow the same process.

So for example, with, uh, when they go and check, um, a vehicle inspector, they give them a theory test, and then they'll watch them do a complete vehicle inspection to see how well they're doing it with the inspecting organization. It's more about looking at the records that they're keeping. And so they look at those in, in quite a lot of detail.

So that inspection might take, you know, if you do one inspector and inspecting organization at the same time, probably takes about half a day. Mm-hmm. Um, I don't think we had any concerns that they weren't doing those to the required standard. There were some, um, challenges, particularly in Auckland for NZTA to keep up with the demand there.

Um, they had a sort of bit more of a backlog in Auckland, but they were taking some, some steps to try and address that. So one of the things was they were taking people from some of the other regions and just supplementing the Auckland staff, um, to try and, um, build that up. And one of the other things they were starting to do, so at the moment, everyone who works on the NZTA side has to be an experienced vehicle inspector, but some of the work, especially for inspector organizations, is looking at paperwork, um,

whether it complies, you don't need to be a mechanic to do that. So they're looking at getting other people to do some of that part of the work so that the people with the mechanical expertise can just focus on, on that side of it. Can I ask more? The, the reason I'm asking that, because I think there's a major concern from, from the general topics that, you know, different garage or different repair, they have different standard, if I bring the same car mm-hmm.

I may pass on, on, on, on shop A, but I fail on B and C, like, so that, that actually bring another concern is whether those report are truly reflect the, the, the car status or the safety, um, the safety of the car as well. So how, how, how can we adjust that and how can also we, um, PFA, like a lot of people tell me that they, they don't want to go to do the wharf because every single time they go there, the mechanic will find something they they want to fix.

How can we PFA those, uh, is there anything you, you find out is unnecessary repair, uh, uh, being mm-hmm. You know, implement during those, um, inspection process as well? So apart from the regular, the regular visits that NZTA does, the main thing was the data analysis, um, that we talked about. So, you know, they see a, a, a particular individual or inspecting organization that's got a higher, um, fail rate or even a higher pass rate than the average, they would look into that to see if there's a pattern.

Mm-hmm. Um, that suggests, you know, suggests that perhaps, um, they are failing at planning someone too often, um, or on things un unnecessarily. I think, you know, as, as a vehicle owner, as a consumer, you've got the right to shop around if you're not happy, um, with the service that you're getting when you go and get a warrant of fitness, you can, you can go somewhere else.

You can also, um, make a complaint, um, to the garage directly. A lot of vehicle inspectors, um, are part of a wider organization. So some are members of MTA, the Motor Trades Association, others are you vehicle testing organizations. So there's a bigger, um, organization that you can complain to, and ultimately you can also make a complaint to NZTA and if they think there's grounds for complaint, they'll look into that.

Cool. Just following Up on that. So, so what you're saying is that the NZTA inspectors are members of, so a a they're all sort of, they're qualified in, in the field, obviously they, so they know what they're doing. Um, they're, they're members of various different, um, Organizations. No. So the NNTA people just work for NZTA, so they're independent, but if as you, if you go to the garage, the garage is probably the, and a member of some, So my question there was, um, are you happy that NZ TA's, people are keeping their skills up to scratch?

In other words, they know what they're doing, particularly as vehicles evolve. Mm-hmm. So the NZT inspectors are knowing what they're supposed to be looking for and that they're doing a, a fair and reasonable job. Yeah. We didn't look at that in a lot of detail, but, um, yeah, we didn't see any concerns about, um, about the skills of the NZTA people.

So you, we didn't see any concerns Yeah. With that. Yeah. So if we were talking to NZTA, that'd be something. So how not have you got a problem, but how do you make sure that your, um, your team are kept up to up speed, especially as cars keep changing or vehicles keep Track? I, I think the bigger problem that we heard about was that there's actually a shortage of people with the inspection skills.

So both working in the industry and then that ma there's a challenge for NZTA to recruit enough people. Um, so there's a lot of, um, a lot of inspectors now coming from overseas because they've had to, um, you know, there's just not enough people in New Zealand with the skills and wanting to do that role. That's interesting. Mm-hmm. Um, the other question is just we, we touched a little bit on, on emerging technologies and how those are dealt with.

Mm-hmm. Um, so can you sort of walk us through a little bit more what needs to be done in terms of regulation slash legislation? Obviously there are two diff quite different things, um, uh, to respond to those changes. Mm-hmm. So, I mean, it's not the role of of our office to really say what, what should be done, but I think the challenge that's ahead is that, you know, as I said, at the moment, it's a physical inspection.

Someone does it, it's, it's fairly straightforward. The challenge going forward is something quite different. You've got all these vehicle manufacturers with probably great big, um, teams of software developers who are constantly changing, um, so, and updating software, and it's really not feasible for our government department to be keeping up with all those changes.

So the whole model of how we do and how we, um, regulate for vehicle safety may need to change. Um, so it's not something that's straightforward, but that's, um, the challenge really for the Ministry of Transport to deal with. Yeah. That, and that's why I asked the question about whether you'd have any engagement with the vehicle manufacturers factor because No, some of that stuff I, I, I just, I just had the pleasure of being down the, the winter testing mm-hmm.

Um, environment down south and um, you know, that is a large part. It's about software. Mm-hmm. It's not about hardware, it's about software keeping vehicles on, on the road and keeping them straight and so on. So, but you wouldn't be able to, most inspectors would've no show No. Of being able to work, so that that's the challenge that they've got. Yeah. Yeah. Good. Thanks Mr. Chair. Um, trends overseas in terms of how this industry may look in 20 years time?

Like do, going back to your point, I mean, we've got an electric vehicle and the manufacturer provides updates remotely. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. Um, so I guess the question is do we get to a point where actually, um, you know, they're so autonomous, um, tech enabled that, that, um, inspections could almost happen remotely, you know, to identify okay, what are the weak areas of the vehicle?

Mm-hmm. Like, do is think that that is something other jurisdiction looking at? Or, or is this kind of pie the sky science fiction? Um, again, I think it's probably we, not for us to say what that might look like, but I, I guess that is a possibility that that might be one of the ways that, you know, you get that assurance about vehicle safety in the future.

Yeah. You know, making sure that you are, you know, at the moment no one checks that your vehicle has had the software updates that it needs. Mm-hmm. Um, but I mean there's, what I guess I'm getting at is we've got physical, our industry at the moment is based on physical people that check everything. Yeah. I guess the question is, uh, is there ever a moment where potentially, um, you know, vehicle testers are able to remotely test, uh, vehicles, um, but plugging in and, you know, I mean, I would say potentially, but we couldn't say whether that would actually

include whether that's gonna work or not. Okay. Thank You. It's definitely, it's definitely a question for the Ministry of Transport. In, in 2022, they had the long-term insights briefing that highlighted these issues. So I believe the undertaking work, the undertaking work at beginning of this year. So, um, around the, and so if I, if I was, um, suggesting something to the committee, it would be our recommendations three and five really, um, which three is around, goes to the point around the regulation and how it's making sure that the, that they understand the,

the Warren fitness is targeted towards the greatest risks, um, and they understand the impact of these things. And then the five, making sure that the rules can change at the same speed of, um, as the technology changes, all of which the ministry and NZTA are, um, on top of Mm-hmm. Thank you Guys. Is just how you mentioned, in order to regulate the industry, you actually look at the data or how many wolf being passed and fail.

So how can we know that those data will receive from NAT are the truly reflect what's happening on, on the ground? For example, I think a lot of people will go to bring their cars there. Mm-hmm. And, and the, the mechanic will give me a course of a Carlos, you can't pass the W if you don't change this.

So, and, and, and I just don't believe that they actually launch into the dataset I failed my car. So they, they, things can be already fixed before it's actually input into the data. So that will, so is that not the, not the very, uh, the, the data we collect mm-hmm. Will not truly be fed what's going on in in real life.

Yes. There is certainly a requirement that, um, Vic on spec should be recording. Um, any failures, um, that should be something that they're putting into the system and that's something I think they would check. Um, you know, when they look at the records, they would see ones that have failed. They would know, they'd be looking to see that they have, have failed.

So if you had a a, I guess it's the flip side, that if the data was showing that a garage had too high a pass rate, they'd be suggesting that, well, what's happening to the ones that that failed? 'cause if some garages look like they're passing more cars than others, that would be suspicious because you'd expect a certain level, level of failures to be recorded.

So I think that would be picked up in the data data that way. Yeah. So, So this this's what I'm saying, like the way we collecting the data right now mm-hmm. The model, we, we collect the data right now, it can be manipulated by, by the mechanic. Mm-hmm. So it's, so data we collect will not be really accurate, But the data would show if, um, if they're not recording a failure, then their pass rate would be too high.

So although the, the failure will be in the system, the overall, um, pass rates for that inspector or that garage might look suspiciously high. They haven't recorded the ones that they've failed. And so that would potentially pick that up. So will individual mechanic know what is too high or too low? Will they know what's the numbers, like the percentage too high to low?

Do they know? Because otherwise I can monitor, query my data to, to fit into that range as well. I think, you know, that, as I said, it's not a, not a perfect system, but I think, um, you know, the, the sorts of analysis that NZTA does will pick up some of those things because it will pick up missing data. You know, in terms of if they're manipulating the data, then that's where it's really down to the, the owner if they think that something's not being done properly. Um, yeah.

So, so just now you've mentioned this is not a perfect system. So from your point of view, how can we improve that system so we can truly effect, you know, the data is truly effect what's going on? Is it, is there any idea or, or, I, I think the data is probably, probably the key. So that, as I said, they do some data analysis at the moment, what we think they could do is expand on that data analysis and that would help if they had, um, improved and updated IT systems.

But, you know, they use some various measures that we've talked about, so pass some fail rates as a few other ones that they use, but they could cut that data in different ways and perhaps dig into it in different ways to see where there are more risks. Um, you know, what sorts of inspectors or inspecting organizations perhaps have a higher risk of non-compliance and look at those in in more detail.

But really, um, you know, when you've got, as you said, quite a small number of in inspect of people at NZTA and a big population of vehicle inspectors and the data's one way that it's quite efficient if you use that data well of, um, you know, of picking up where there are problems. Sure. Just In terms of the recommendations, um, this, this report was February, um, so obviously been some, um, potential for NCTA and MOT to respond.

So what's their, you know, when they've got the report, what's the feedback they've been that they've given you and, and what have they done as a response to it? So, I mean, the response at the time was they were supportive of the findings. Um, we haven't talked to them about it since, since then. Um, but we do have a standard follow up procedure, so probably within another 12 to 18 months we would go back to them formally 18 months as a follow up and ask them about the progress that they've made.

And, um, we'll publish that information. Yeah. What would you suggest that we, that we should do with this in terms of following it up? Because the, 'cause we will obviously go 'cause mm-hmm. A lot of these things you said, well, that's actually their business rather than you're Yeah. You know, you're around their system, um, about how the system's actually working, um, and particularly some of those things about the new technologies, et cetera, as well as the, as well as the mm-hmm.

Mm-hmm. The inspecting the inspector system, um, and responding to your recommendations. So what, what would, what would your recommendation to us be in terms of what we should do? Yes. I think, as Jason said, probably the recommendations three and and five are probably the key ones. And, you know, there's obviously been a lot of interest with the committee, um, about the, you know, the challenges of the technology and, and changing technology and what that means.

So that might be something you could follow up with probably the Ministry of Transport on that one of what progress they're making with that policy work.
